draft.introduction: Peter’s confession at Caesarea Philippi is the turning point of Mark’s Gospel. Jesus asks who people say he is, Peter answers “the Christ,” and Jesus immediately predicts his suffering and death.

translation.text: And Jesus went on with his disciples to the villages of Caesarea Philippi. And on the way he asked his disciples, “Who do people say that I am?” And they told him, “John the Baptist; and others say, Elijah; and others, one of the prophets.” And he asked them, “But who do you say that I am?” Peter answered him, “You are the Christ.”
translation.original_text: καὶ ἐξῆλθεν ὁ Ἰησοῦς καὶ οἱ μαθηταὶ αὐτοῦ

translation.text: And he began to teach them that the Son of Man must suffer many things and be rejected by the elders and the chief priests and the scribes and be killed, and after three days rise again. And Peter took him aside and began to rebuke him. But he rebuked Peter and said, “Get behind me, Satan! For you are not setting your mind on the things of God.”

translator_notes.note: Mark’s version of the confession is notably shorter than Matthew’s—Peter says only “You are the Christ” without “the Son of the living God.” Jesus’ immediate command to silence suggests that the title, while correct, is dangerously incomplete without the suffering that follows.
